examples.title: Check 1NF Compliance
examples.description: Algorithm to verify if a relation satisfies First Normal Form
examples.code:
FUNCTION isIn1NF(relation R)
BEGIN
    FOR EACH attribute A IN R
    BEGIN
        FOR EACH tuple t IN R
        BEGIN
            IF NOT isAtomic(t[A]) THEN
                RETURN FALSE
            END
        END
    END
    RETURN TRUE
END

FUNCTION isAtomic(value v)
BEGIN
    IF v IS NULL THEN RETURN TRUE
    IF v IS LIST OR v IS ARRAY OR v IS SET THEN
        RETURN FALSE
    RETURN TRUE
END
examples.explanation: This algorithm checks each attribute value in every tuple to ensure it contains only atomic (single) values. Non-atomic values include lists, arrays, sets, or any composite data.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m) where n is number of tuples, m is number of attributes

examples.title: Find Candidate Keys
examples.description: Algorithm to find all candidate keys from functional dependencies
examples.explanation: This Python code finds all candidate keys by computing attribute closures for all possible attribute combinations and checking if they are minimal superkeys.
examples.timeComplexity: O(2^n × n × m) exponential in number of attributes

examples.title: Normalize to 3NF (Synthesis Algorithm)
examples.description: Algorithm to decompose a relation into 3NF using the synthesis approach
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Synthesize3NF
INPUT: Relation R, set of FDs F
OUTPUT: Set of relations in 3NF

BEGIN
    // Step 1: Find minimal cover of FDs
    F_min = MinimalCover(F)
    
    // Step 2: Create relation for each FD in minimal cover
    Relations = []
    FOR EACH FD (X → Y) IN F_min
    BEGIN
        R_new = X ∪ Y
        IF NOT ContainsRelation(Relations, R_new) THEN
            Add Relations, R_new
    END
    
    // Step 3: If no relation contains a candidate key, add one
    CK = FindCandidateKey(R, F)
    IF NOT AnyRelationContains(Relations, CK) THEN
        Add Relations, CK
    
    // Step 4: Remove relations that are subsets of others
    Relations = RemoveRedundant(Relations)
    
    RETURN Relations
END

ALGORITHM MinimalCover
INPUT: Set of FDs F
OUTPUT: Minimal cover of F
BEGIN
    // Split RHS to single attributes
    F1 = []
    FOR EACH FD (X → {A,B,C}) IN F
        F1.append((X, {A})); F1.append((X, {B})); F1.append((X, {C}))
    
    // Remove extraneous LHS attributes
    FOR EACH FD (X → {A}) IN F1
        FOR EACH attribute B IN X
            IF Closure(X - {B}, F1) CONTAINS A THEN
                X = X - {B}
    
    // Remove redundant FDs
    F_min = []
    FOR EACH FD f IN F1
        IF NOT Closure(f.LHS, F1 - {f}) CONTAINS f.RHS THEN
            Add F_min, f
    
    RETURN F_min
END
examples.explanation: The synthesis algorithm transforms a relation into 3NF by first computing the minimal cover of functional dependencies, creating relations for each FD, ensuring a candidate key is preserved, and removing redundant relations.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n × m²) where n is number of FDs, m is number of attributes

examples.title: Check BCNF Violation
examples.description: C program to check if a relation violates BCNF and identify violating FDs
examples.explanation: This C program checks if a relation violates BCNF by computing closures and verifying if each FD's left-hand side is a superkey. It demonstrates the core algorithm used in normalization tools.
examples.timeComplexity: O(f × n × a) where f is number of FDs, n is number of iterations, a is number of attributes
